summary:
Babur (1483–1530 AD) was the founder of the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] — a Timurid prince from Central Asia who, after failing to hold his inherited kingdom of Fergana and repeatedly losing and recapturing Samarkand, turned south toward India and conquered the Delhi Sultanate with a small but technologically superior force at the [[event:2380|First Battle of Panipat]] (1526). Babur was remarkable among conquerors for his literary sensibility: his memoir, the Baburnama (Book of Babur), is one of the first autobiographies in Islamic literature and a vivid, honest, and often self-deprecating account of his campaigns, defeats, loves, and the garden design that was his personal passion. Descended from both Timur (Tamerlane) on his father's side and Genghis Khan on his mother's, he embodied the Central Asian tradition of warrior-scholar princes.

Babur's conquest of India was achieved through the effective use of firearms and field artillery — weapons that the Afghan and Rajput armies of the Delhi Sultanate and its rivals lacked. At Panipat, his artillery and flanking cavalry maneuvers crushed the numerically superior army of Ibrahim Lodi; a year later, he defeated the Rajput confederation at Khanwa (1527). But Babur never fully settled in India — he found its climate oppressive and yearned for Kabul and the melons of Central Asia. He died in 1530, leaving a precarious empire to his son [[event:3233|Humayun]], who would nearly lose it before the dynasty stabilized under [[event:2381|Akbar]]. Babur's legacy is the civilization he founded: the Mughal Empire would govern most of South Asia for over 300 years, blending Persian, Central Asian, and Indian cultural traditions into one of the world's great civilizations.
